items.label: No cappuccino after 11am
items.text: Italians only drink milky coffee in the morning. Ordering one after lunch marks you as a tourist — not offensive, just unusual.

items.label: Espresso is just 'caffè'
items.text: Ask for 'un caffè' and you get an espresso. It's drunk standing at the bar in 30 seconds.

items.label: Ordering at the bar
items.text: Pay at the cassa (till) first, get a receipt, then take it to the barista and say what you want.

items.label: Caffè corretto
items.text: Espresso with a shot of grappa or sambuca — an Italian morning tradition in some regions.

items.label: Caffè shakerato
items.text: Espresso shaken with ice and sugar — the Italian iced coffee. Order this in summer.

items.label: Prices
items.text: Espresso at the bar: €1–1.50. At a table (servizio): double. Tourist areas: triple.

items.label: Antipasto
items.text: Starter — bruschetta, cured meats, olives, seafood

items.label: Primo
items.text: First course — pasta, risotto, soup. This is often the main event.

items.label: Secondo
items.text: Main course — meat or fish. Usually served alone, no sides included.

items.label: Contorno
items.text: Side dish — order separately with your secondo

items.label: Dolce
items.text: Dessert — tiramisù, panna cotta, gelato, cannoli

items.label: Caffè
items.text: Coffee — always after dessert, never with it

items.label: Coperto
items.text: Cover charge (€1.50–4) per person — listed on the menu. Legal and normal.

items.label: Servizio
items.text: Service charge (10–15%) may be included. Check before adding a tip.

items.label: Tipping
items.text: Not obligatory. Leave €1–2 per person for good service — leave cash on the table.

items.label: Rome
items.text: Cacio e pepe, carbonara, amatriciana — the Roman pasta holy trinity

items.label: Naples
items.text: Pizza Margherita — invented here in 1889. Thin base, San Marzano tomatoes, buffalo mozzarella

items.label: Florence
items.text: Bistecca alla Fiorentina — T-bone steak, grilled rare over charcoal, sold by weight

items.label: Tuscany
items.text: Ribollita — hearty bread and bean soup, the original Tuscan peasant food

items.label: Sicily
items.text: Arancini, pasta alla Norma, fresh cannoli — buy from a pasticceria, not a tourist shop

items.label: Emilia-Romagna
items.text: Parmigiano-Reggiano, prosciutto di Parma, mortadella — the food capital of Italy

items.label: Venice
items.text: Sarde in saor, bacalà mantecato, risotto al nero di seppia (squid ink risotto)

items.label: Calabria
items.text: Nduja (spreadable spicy salami), fileja pasta, peperoncino everything

items.label: Bar
items.text: For coffee and breakfast (cornetto + cappuccino). Open from 7am.

items.label: Trattoria
items.text: Family-run, traditional, affordable. No frills, great food. Go here.

items.label: Osteria
items.text: Even more rustic than a trattoria — wine-focused with simple food.

items.label: Ristorante
items.text: Formal and pricier. Better for special occasions than everyday meals.

items.label: Pizzeria
items.text: Order by the whole pizza — sharing is unusual. Eat at the table, not walking.

items.label: Mercato
items.text: Food markets are the best places to eat cheaply and authentically. Ask locals where theirs is.

items.label: Avoid
items.text: Restaurants with pictures on the menu, touts at the door, or 'tourist menu' signs — all red flags.

items.label: Artisanal gelato
items.text: Look for gelato displayed in metal tubs with lids (pozzetti) — this is the real thing. Avoid gelato piled high in mountainous peaks with bright unnatural colours.

items.label: Gusti
items.text: Choose 1–3 flavours (gusti). Classic: pistachio (pistacchio), hazelnut (nocciola), stracciatella.

items.label: Cone or cup?
items.text: Cono (cone) or coppetta (cup). Ask for panna (whipped cream) on top — often free.

items.label: Price
items.text: €2–4 for a small. Pay first at the till in many shops.

items.label: Gelato vs ice cream
items.text: Lower fat, more intense flavour, served slightly warmer. Nothing like supermarket ice cream.

items.label: Vino della casa
items.text: House wine — order a carafe (una caraffa) or half-carafe. Cheap, honest, often very good.

items.label: Aperitivo
items.text: The pre-dinner drink ritual, 6–8pm. Order a Campari Spritz or Aperol Spritz. In Milan many bars serve free nibbles with your aperitivo.

items.label: Digestivo
items.text: After-dinner drink — Limoncello (south), Grappa (north), Amaro. Usually free at the end of a meal in traditional restaurants.

items.label: Acqua frizzante
items.text: Sparkling water — if you want still water, ask for acqua naturale or acqua liscia.

items.label: Regional wines
items.text: Barolo & Barbaresco (Piedmont), Chianti (Tuscany), Brunello di Montalcino, Amarone (Veneto), Nero d'Avola (Sicily)
